Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, but honestly it was always a struggle. I would block out time in the evenings after work, usually around 7 PM, and try to study for three to four hours. Some weeks I managed to stick to it, but when my anxiety got bad, everything fell apart. I learned that listening to recorded lectures worked better for me than reading, so I built my routine around audio materials when I could.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling mentally. When deadlines approached, I would cram in extra hours wherever I could find them. But during calmer weeks, I tried to spread things out more evenly across the days. The problem was that with 13 courses and limited resources, something always seemed to be demanding my attention urgently.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or if grades had been posted. I also logged in when I needed to participate in discussions because I knew those counted toward my participation. Sometimes I would log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even when I was too anxious to actually focus on the material.
Q4: After a few weeks, logging in every morning became almost automatic for me. I would check the LMS with my coffee before starting my day, just to see what was happening. The peer support group I was connected with helped make it feel less isolating, so I actually wanted to check in and see what others were posting. It became part of my daily rhythm even on bad days.
Q5: The discussion forums and peer interactions were genuinely helpful for me. Hearing how other students approached problems made concepts click in ways that reading alone never did. I also relied heavily on any audio or video content because that matches how I learn best. When classmates would explain things in their own words, it often made more sense than the official materials.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings and some of the longer video lectures. With limited time and resources, I had to prioritize what would directly help me pass. The optional practice quizzes I sometimes ignored because they felt overwhelming when I was already stressed. I knew they would help, but I just couldn't add more to my plate.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. I also valued anything that helped me understand concepts I was struggling with, especially when peers were involved. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grades, I had a hard time justifying the time investment given everything else I was juggling.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required activities had to get done no matter how I was feeling, so I pushed through. With optional ones, my anxiety would tell me I should do them, but my exhaustion would win out. There was this constant guilt about not doing more, but I had to make peace with just surviving sometimes.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline. I would download lecture recordings and listen to them on my commute or while doing chores. I also borrowed textbooks from the library since I couldn't afford to buy them all. A lot of my actual learning happened while I was walking around with headphones in, replaying explanations until they stuck.
Q10: There were many times when I was actively studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. I would download a PDF once and then review it multiple times on my phone or printed copies. I also met with study partners in person, which was some of my most productive learning time. The system only saw me download something once, not the hours I spent with it afterward.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy for a lot of materials. I would access a video or document once, download it, and then work with it offline for days. With my internet being unreliable at home sometimes, this was necessary. I got comfortable working with materials in offline mode and only syncing up when I needed to submit something or check discussions.
Q12: There were several weeks when my anxiety peaked and everything felt impossible. I was dealing with financial stress and some family issues that made it hard to concentrate. During those times, even logging in felt overwhelming because I knew I was behind. The sadness would settle in and make even simple tasks feel like climbing a mountain.
Q13: Definitely, all of those factors affected my LMS use significantly. When I was exhausted from work, I would log in but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Confusion about course material made me avoid certain sections entirely until I could get help from peers. The competing responsibilities of work and family meant the LMS often got my leftover energy, which wasn't much.
Q14: Many times I would open materials and read the same paragraph over and over without anything registering. My body was there, my eyes were moving, but my mind was somewhere else worrying about bills or other problems. I would watch videos at 2x speed just to check them off, knowing I wasn't really absorbing the content. It felt hollow but I didn't know what else to do.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation a lot because it triggered my anxiety the most. The thought of failing would paralyze me, so I would do other assignments instead to feel productive. I knew the exams were important, which is probably why my exam scores weren't as strong as my assignment completion. It's a self-defeating cycle I'm still trying to break.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire existence in this course. I would check the calendar constantly and organize my week around what was due soonest. The pressure helped me focus when my motivation was low, but it also spiked my stress levels significantly. I became very good at calculating exactly how much time I needed to complete something adequately.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would motivate me for a day or two, but negative feedback would send me into a spiral of self-doubt. I started checking grades less frequently to protect my mental health. When I did get constructive criticism, I tried to use it, but sometimes it just added to my feeling of not being good enough.
Q18: The course structure with clear deadlines and organized modules helped me stay on track when I was struggling. Having everything laid out meant I could plan ahead even when I felt overwhelmed. But the sheer volume of 13 courses meant I was always juggling, and this course sometimes got less attention than it deserved. The peer support intervention was actually really helpful for keeping me connected.
Q19: The discussion forums and peer interaction features made engagement easier because I learn well through conversation. However, some of the reading-heavy modules were harder for me as an auditory learner. When materials were only available in text format, I would struggle more. More audio options or recorded explanations would have helped me engage better with difficult concepts.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is desperately trying to understand something but struggling. They might be clicking through materials repeatedly, searching for explanations that make sense, but unable to produce quality work. It could also be anxiety-driven behavior, where you feel like you need to be doing something but can't focus enough to actually complete assignments. I've been close to that state myself.
Q21: That definitely resonates with my experience somewhat. A lot of my real learning happened offline, through downloaded materials and study groups that wouldn't show up in the system. Someone could have low LMS activity but be studying extensively with printed notes or borrowed textbooks. The platform only captures one slice of how learning actually happens.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone is logging in and what they're clicking on, which gives a pattern of behavior. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and whether learning is actually happening. They can't capture offline study, peer conversations, or the emotional state behind the clicks. A student could look highly engaged in the data while actually being in crisis.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious about a topic and want to discuss it with others, when I'm thinking about course concepts even outside of study time. Real engagement feels energizing even when it's challenging. But honestly, with my anxiety and stress, I've had to redefine engagement as just showing up and trying, even when it's hard.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much emotional energy it took for me to complete each task, or the anxiety attacks I worked through to submit assignments on time. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that kept me from dropping out, or the hours I spent listening to lectures while walking to calm my nerves. My 99.8% assignment completion rate doesn't show that some of those submissions happened at 3 AM after hours of panic. The data shows I passed, but not what it cost me.
